name: Isimud
description: Isimud, also known as Usmu, was a minor Sumerian deity, serving primarily as the two-faced (or Janus-faced) messenger and attendant of Enki (Ea), the god of wisdom, magic, and fresh water. His dual faces allowed him to see both the front and back of situations, making him an insightful and trustworthy servant. He was depicted with two faces looking in opposite directions and was often associated with waterways and the transmission of divine knowledge. Isimud acted as a bridge between the gods and humans, conveying messages and facilitating communication. Though not a major god in his own right, Isimud played a crucial role in the myths surrounding Enki, particularly in the tales of Enki's dealings with humanity and the other gods. He is a symbol of duality, perception, and the importance of clear communication in both the divine and mortal realms. He possesses no great powers of his own, but his loyalty to Enki grants him a degree of authority and access to the god's vast wisdom and magical abilities. Isimud's existence underscores the significance of messengers and intermediaries in ancient Sumerian society and religion. His presence is a reminder of the need for clear communication and understanding in all aspects of life.
subtitle: Two-faced Messenger of Enki

keyMyths.title: Isimud's Role in Enki and Ninhursag
keyMyths.description: Isimud acts as Enki's messenger in the myth of Enki and Ninhursag, conveying messages between the two deities. This myth highlights his role as a facilitator of communication and a loyal servant.
keyMyths.source: The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature

keyMyths.title: Attendant of Enki
keyMyths.description: Isimud is a constant attendant of Enki, assisting him in his various endeavors and ensuring the smooth execution of his plans. This emphasizes his dedication and reliability.
keyMyths.source: Sumerian Mythology: A Study of Spiritual and Literary Achievement in the Third Millennium B.C.

keyMyths.title: Transmission of Knowledge
keyMyths.description: Isimud's connection to Enki, the god of wisdom, implies that he is involved in the transmission of knowledge to both gods and humans. He is a conduit for divine understanding.
keyMyths.source: Gods, Demons and Symbols of Ancient Mesopotamia

sources.author: Samuel Noah Kramer
sources.work: Sumerian Mythology: A Study of Spiritual and Literary Achievement in the Third Millennium B.C.

sources.text: Provides a general overview of Sumerian mythology and the role of various deities.
